replies.author: @JEREMY99218
replies.text: At a school like ODU, there's a good chance the ROTC cadet is a prior service veteran and/or enrolled in the Simultaneous Membership Program, where he is in ROTC and in the Army Reserve or National Guard. Therefore, eligible for regular Army awards.

author: @GorillaGamingPSN
text: Its Bronze Star with V device not 5 which means you earned the award for Valor.  A V device on a ribbon means Valor for those that don't know.  There are a series of robbon devices like oak cluster for multiple awards, knots, C, R, Stars etc.  The only award that does not receiver a device is the Medal of honor all the other service medals and awards can have a device or multiple devices depending on the circumstance in which the award was earned.

author: @ZeroFighter
text:
6:00
When I was in high school- Old Dominion is a university, so one step beyond high school, keep that in mind- our JROTC (Junior Reserve Officers Training Corps) taught us to use ballistic shields in formations to protect ourselves, our fellow students, teachers, and those in the neighboring elementary school just a short walk down the block in the event of a shooter. Other than shielding people, we were told to avoid gunfire if possible, but if we had no choice, they also trained us in disarming and taking down people in hand-to-hand combat. There's nothing fancy to what they taught us; just moving in shield formations, and using mechanical leverage to disarm a person, bring them to the ground, and keep them facedown in the dirt with as many of us cadets on top of them as can fit. The did not train us with weapons outside of summer camps on bases, and even that was highly restricted; no explosives, blanks only, no live rounds, etc...

ROTC in college / universities are that step above high school JROTC. They handle real weapons off-campus, and they train for actual, active military service. They're going to teach more than just using shields to get people away from the danger; they teach how to END threats.

author: @larry648
text: A very sad day for me. When I first retired, I worked armed EP security at this location.  This shooting could have been me. During Covid I left for something else.  My friends still work this detail. They are good.  I’m a catholic, but I really love my Jewish friends after working for them. They treated me wonderfully, loved having me there, and fed me well. I respected them and asked the rabbi what I should do to be respectful.  He told me I didn’t need to do anything, but I did. I always wore a black suit and a proper hat, just out of respect.
